raw_data:
b' Matt Levine's Money Stuff
As a first approximation, if you are the chief executive officer of a big public company, you can do whatever you want. You are the boss, yo

Bad mergers

As a first approximation, if you are the chief executive officer of a big public company, you can do whatever you want. You are the boss, you write the checks, and if you want to spend your company\xe2\x80\x99s money on buying yourself a nicer office chair or upgrading the corporate jet, you can. If you are the CEO of a ball bearings company and you want to open a film studio so that you can hang out with movie stars, sure, you\xe2\x80\x99re the boss. Or if you are the CEO of a payments company and you want to buy Jay-Z\xe2\x80\x99s music streaming company because you like hanging out with Jay-Z, you can do that.

This is only a first approximation, of course, and there are\xc2\xa0constraints. There are legal constraints: You can\xe2\x80\x99t do crimes, or conceal what you\xe2\x80\x99re doing from your board, or take bribes. But the main constraints are probably something like:

  1. You have a board of directors, and they are technically your boss, and they can fire you if you do too much dumb stuff.
  2. Some big decisions \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0mergers, for instance \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0require board approval, so you can\xe2\x80\x99t even do big dumb stuff unless they sign off first.
  3. You have shareholders, and if you do too much dumb stuff, they might kick you out. ( In practice this means that an activist shareholder might win a proxy fight to kick you out, or a hostile acquirer might buy your company to kick you out.)
  4. You are a shareholder, and if you do too much dumb stuff your shares will lose value and you will be poorer.
  5. You probably got to where you are, being the CEO of a public company, by internalizing an ethos of shareholder value maximization, and you probably want to do right by shareholders. Sure you\xe2\x80\x99ll buy yourself a nice office chair, but you will make big decisions by asking yourself \xe2\x80\x9cis this best for shareholders,\xe2\x80\x9d and if you don\xe2\x80\x99t have a good answer you won\xe2\x80\x99t do it. [1]

I suspect that No. 5 is the main constraint for most CEOs, most of the time. But it is worth saying that some CEOs are not really subject to some of these constraints. Some CEOs \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0particularly charismatic founder-CEOs who were not socialized in an ethos of shareholder value \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0might not care about doing right by shareholders. They might own a lot of stock, or super-voting stock, in the company, making activism or hostile takeovers unlikely. And while a lot of their net worth might be tied up in their company, they might be rich and weird enough not to care very much about how their decisions affect their personal wealth.

So then the main constraint is the board. [2] If you are the CEO of a payments company and you dial in to a board meeting from your Hamptons vacation with Jay-Z and say \xe2\x80\x9chey, I\xe2\x80\x99m here with Jay-Z, he\xe2\x80\x99s got a music streaming company, I think we should buy it,\xe2\x80\x9d it\xe2\x80\x99s your board\xe2\x80\x99s job to tell you no.\xc2\xa0But what if they tell you yes?

In fact, Block Inc. ( formerly Square Inc. ), the payments company run by Jack Dorsey, acquired Tidal, the music streaming company run by Shawn Carter (Jay-Z), in April 2021. Dorsey is rich (even outside of his Block shares), owns super-voting Block stock , and, uh, is not a conventional shareholder-value-maximizing public-company CEO . Here is an incredible summary of how the deal came about:

Block facilitates payment processing and helps individuals transfer money electronically; it had never ventured into the music streaming industry and, at the time it acquired TIDAL, had no plans to do so. The idea for the acquisition came to Jack Dorsey\xe2\x80\x94Block\xe2\x80\x99s founder, CEO, and Chairman\xe2\x80\x94when he was summering with Carter in the Hamptons. From his Hamptons retreat, Dorsey joined a videoconference meeting of Block\xe2\x80\x99s board and proposed that Block acquire TIDAL. The board formed a transaction committee to consider the proposal.

Over the ensuing months, the committee learned that TIDAL was failing financially, losing its major contracts, and facing an ongoing criminal investigation. The committee also learned that Carter personally loaned TIDAL $50 million to help the troubled company through its difficulties and that Dorsey was the sole Block management member in support of the acquisition. Despite the obvious problems with the deal, the committee approved the transaction for $306 million. It seemed, by all accounts, a terrible business decision.

Shareholders sued, and that summary is from\xc2\xa0the opening of a Delaware Chancery Court opinion in the case yesterday. The shareholders lost! The opinion goes on:

Under Delaware law, however, a board comprised of a majority of disinterested and independent directors is free to make a terrible business decision without any meaningful threat of liability, so long as the directors approve the action in good faith.

The important point here is that courts in Delaware \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0where many US public companies are incorporated \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0do not want to be in the business of deciding whether any particular business decision is terrible. So Delaware has a \xe2\x80\x9cbusiness judgment rule,\xe2\x80\x9d in which courts try not to second-guess the business judgments of corporate directors. This rule is not absolute: The directors have a duty of care and a duty of loyalty to shareholders; if they have a conflict of interest, or if they are incredibly lazy and careless, a court might review their decisions and hold them liable for breaching their duties. But if they have no conflicts of interest and more or less act like directors , and their decisions are terrible, courts won\xe2\x80\x99t do anything about it. [3]

Delaware is proud of this rule; this rule is not just \xe2\x80\x9cwhaddarya gonna do sometimes boards do bad stuff,\xe2\x80\x9d but a positively good thing. Letting boards \xe2\x80\x9cmake a terrible business decision without any meaningful threat of liability\xe2\x80\x9d allows companies to take risks, to make business decisions based on their honest assessment of what is best for shareholders rather than on fear of liability.

And so when a Delaware judge \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0here, Chancellor Kathaleen McCormick \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0gets a case like this, and decides that the directors should not be liable, she does not write an opinion saying \xe2\x80\x9cehh this was all fine, it\xe2\x80\x99s not as bad as you think.\xe2\x80\x9d She writes an opinion saying \xe2\x80\x9clol this was all ridiculous, but even so I\xe2\x80\x99m going to allow it, because around here we defer to independent boards of directors.\xe2\x80\x9d

So the opinion is not kind to Block\xe2\x80\x99s directors:

Plaintiff alleges that Dorsey caused Block to submit a letter of intent to purchase TIDAL before the Transaction Committee was even formed. The Transaction Committee then allowed Dorsey to handle negotiations. After discussing the opportunity for only thirty-five minutes during their first meeting in September, the Transaction Committee encouraged Dorsey to move forward. In advance of its second meeting in October, management provided a report that showed just how dire TIDAL\xe2\x80\x99s market position looked.

But the standard for liability is high, and shareholders can only sue if the directors approved the deal in bad faith.\xc2\xa0One way to prove that is with a conflict of interest. And for Dorsey \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0who is the chairman of Block\xe2\x80\x99s board \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0that seems plausible. The conflict of interest is that he spent shareholder money to\xc2\xa0get to hang out with Jay-Z: \xe2\x80\x9cIt is reasonably conceivable that Dorsey used corporate coffers to bolster his relationship with Carter.\xe2\x80\x9d (At Bloomberg Law, Mike Leonard notes that \xe2\x80\x9canalysts blasted the deal as \xe2\x80\x98a $300 million bar tab to hang out with Jay-Z,\xe2\x80\x99\xe2\x80\x9d and Block\xe2\x80\x99s stock dropped 7% when it was announced.)

But the other directors voted to approve the deal, and they didn\xe2\x80\x99t get to hang out with Jay-Z. Instead the question for them is whether they \xe2\x80\x9cconsciously and intentionally disregarded their responsibilities, adopting a \xe2\x80\x98we don\xe2\x80\x99t care about the risk\xe2\x80\x99 attitude concerning a material corporate decision,\xe2\x80\x9d whether they \xe2\x80\x9cknew that they were making material decisions without adequate information and without adequate deliberation, and that they simply did not care if the decisions caused the corporation and its stockholders to suffer injury or loss.\xe2\x80\x9d [4] If they approved the deal carefully enough , then the deal is allowed, even if the CEO was conflicted in bringing it to them.

And they approved it carefully enough:

Plaintiff does not allege that the Transaction Committee lacked a business reason for wanting to acquire TIDAL\xe2\x80\x94the presentation materials show management\xe2\x80\x99s\xc2\xa0strategic goals for expanding Block into the music industry.\xc2\xa0Plaintiff does not allege that any of the Committee Defendants were in any way beholden to Dorsey. Plaintiff acknowledge that the Committee Defendants did not sit idly by while Dorsey presented. They asked many appropriate questions before the October 20 meeting, and they asked many appropriate follow-up questions in advance of the next meeting on January 22. The Transaction Committee were presented with over twenty single-spaced slides providing management\xe2\x80\x99s detailed answers to each of these questions.

Now, sure, those answers were bad:

As Plaintiff sees it, the problem was not that the Transaction Committee failed to ask questions\xe2\x80\x94it is that the answers did not seem to matter.

Before its October meeting, the Transaction Committee asked whether any other members of senior management supported the acquisition; in response, the committee learned that there were none, aside from Dorsey. The Transaction Committee asked whether the artist commitments, which formed the basis for at least half of management\xe2\x80\x99s valuation of TIDAL, were legally enforceable; in response, the committee learned that Block would have \xe2\x80\x9cno recourse\xe2\x80\x9d if the artists decided to walk away. The Transaction Committee asked for near- and long-term plans for integrating TIDAL into Block\xe2\x80\x99s business; in response, the committee learned that management had not created these plans and that this remained \xe2\x80\x9cone of the biggest risks.\xe2\x80\x9d

After the October meeting, the Transaction Committee went dark for three months while Dorsey negotiated the purchase price. Ultimately, without any further meetings, the Transaction Committee approved the acquisition by unanimous written consent.

But that\xe2\x80\x99s all fine: \xe2\x80\x9cAlthough the facts emphasized by Plaintiff do not generate tremendous confidence in the Transaction Committee\xe2\x80\x99s process, they fall short of supporting an inference of bad faith.\xe2\x80\x9d (Also, to be fair, the deal was small \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x9cManagement also responded to
questions about the acquisition\xe2\x80\x99s scale, noting that a $350 million purchase price would only constitute 0.35% of Block\xe2\x80\x99s $100 billion market capitalization\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0so it was not unreasonable for the board not to spend too much time on it.) The process was good enough \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0the board asked questions and got answers \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0even if the results were bad. The law can make boards of directors check the right boxes, but\xc2\xa0it can\xe2\x80\x99t make them make the right decisions, and it doesn\xe2\x80\x99t try. [5]

First Citizens Valley

In March, First Citizens BancShares Inc. bought most of Silicon Valley Bank from the Federal Deposit Insurance Corp., which had seized SVB when it failed earlier that month. The price that First Citizens paid for SVB was, loosely speaking, negative $16.5 billion: It took over about $110.1 billion of SVB\xe2\x80\x99s assets and assumed $93.6 billion of liabilities, for a discount of $16.5 billion.

Today, First Citizens reported earnings for the first quarter , and the way accounting works is pretty much that that discount counts as earnings. Not all of it: First Citizens has some reason to be conservative, [6] to report that actually the assets were worth a bit less than the $110.1 billion headline valuation, and the gain will be reduced by taxes. But most of it: First Citizens reported an after-tax gain on acquisition of $9.82 billion, [7] giving it a total net income for the quarter of $9.52 billion. [8] That is a very good quarter, if you take it literally :

First Citizens Bank, the US lender that acquired much of Silicon Valley Bank following its collapse, reported a more than 30-fold increase in profits for the first three months of 2023, benefiting from its purchase of the failed California-based lender.

For the first quarter, First Citizens said on Wednesday that net income totalled $9.5bn, or $653.64 a share, up from $264mn, or $16.70 a share, in the same period last year, due to a $9.8bn gain from its deal for SVB.

The windfall made First Citizens the second-most profitable bank in the US during the quarter, just behind JPMorgan Chase that earned $12.6bn.

You could imagine , reading a headline like \xe2\x80\x9cFirst Citizens Buys $110 Billion of SVB Assets at $16.5 Billion Discount,\xe2\x80\x9d that the $110 billion number was fake, and that the \xe2\x80\x9cdiscount\xe2\x80\x9d would reflect the actual value of the assets . You could imagine that the FDIC went to First Citizens and said \xe2\x80\x9chey we got $110 billion of SVB stuff, you want it,\xe2\x80\x9d and First Citizens was like \xe2\x80\x9chmm I don\xe2\x80\x99t believe you for a minute that that stuff is worth $110 billion, but we\xe2\x80\x99ll give you $93.6\xc2\xa0billion for it,\xe2\x80\x9d and the FDIC was like \xe2\x80\x9cyeah fine that\xe2\x80\x99s fair.\xe2\x80\x9d There is a lot of that sort of thing going around in US regional banking recently; we have talked about banks ( including SVB! ) that had, like, $120 billion of stuff on their balance sheet that turned out to be worth only $105 billion.

But the point I want to make here is that that\xe2\x80\x99s not what this discount is. First Citizens bought $110 billion of SVB assets that, by its own best estimates, with the benefit of hindsight and and weeks of rigorous valuation review, were worth like $107 billion. And it paid about $94 billion for them; it got roughly a $13 billion discount to actual market values. [9]

Is that not a little weird? The FDIC conducted a (rather lengthy) auction for SVB\xe2\x80\x99s assets, and the best bidder was willing to pay about $13 billion less than they were worth, and the FDIC was fine with that. Why?

Well, we have talked about this too. Last week I wrote , about JPMorgan Chase & Co.\xe2\x80\x99s deal to buy First Republic Bank:

Banks are required \xe2\x80\x94 by regulation but also by common sense \xe2\x80\x94 to have capital , that is, shareholders\xe2\x80\x99 equity, assets that exceed their liabilities. The buyer bank also has to have assets that exceed its liabilities, to have capital against the assets that it buys. If it is buying $85 of loans, it will want to fund them with no more than, say, $75 of liabilities. If it is assuming $90 of deposits, it will have to pay, like, negative $15 for them, which means something like \xe2\x80\x9cthe FDIC gives the buyer $15 to take over the failed bank.\xe2\x80\x9d

If the SVB assets here were worth $107 billion, and First Citizens paid for them by assuming $107 billion of liabilities, it would need billions of dollars of extra capital, call it an extra $10 billion to target a 10% capital ratio. This would require it to go out and raise capital, to sell enormous piles of stock (First Citizens\xe2\x80\x99s total market capitalization is about $16 billion or so) in a rough period for regional banks. Not gonna work.

Instead, the FDIC effectively gave it the capital: It sold First Citizens $107 billion of assets for $94 billion, giving it an extra $10 billion of capital (after tax) to support those assets and remain well capitalized. Actually it got better capitalized: First Citizens\xe2\x80\x99s common equity tier 1 capital ratio at the end of 2022 was about 10.1%; at the end of the first quarter of 2023 it was about 12.5%. [10]

The FDIC\xe2\x80\x99s insurance fund took a big loss on SVB, but the loss was not driven entirely by the fact that SVB\xe2\x80\x99s deposits were bigger than the market value of its assets. The loss also came from the fact that the FDIC was paying to recapitalize the banking system . The problem was that SVB\xe2\x80\x99s losses left it undercapitalized, and the fix was to hand SVB to some other bank and make it well-capitalized. And that costs money.

Elsewhere in bank earnings

We have talked a bunch recently about two different ways of looking at banks and interest-rate risk. One theory, which I have called Theory 1, is that banks have short-term liabilities (deposits) and long-term assets (bonds, loans), and so when interest rates go up their costs go up and their assets lose value: Banks are at risk of bad things if interest rates go up. This theory is plainly correct as applied to Silicon Valley Bank\xc2\xa0or First Republic, both of which disappeared because rising rates left them insolvent.

The other theory, which I have called Theory 2, is that banks actually have long-term liabilities (because their depositors are loyal and not very interest-rate-sensitive) matched with long-term assets, and that when interest rates go up banks make more money by charging more for loans without paying much more for deposits: Banks benefit from raising interest rates. This theory seems correct as applied to most of the other banks. At the Financial Times, Stephen Gandel reports :

Profits in the US banking sector reached an all-time high of roughly $80bn in the first quarter, up 33 per cent from a year ago, even as the industry contended with the aftermath of two bank failures and the most significant stress since the 2008 financial crisis.

The banking turmoil was in large part responsible for the bumper haul. About half of the increase in the industry\xe2\x80\x99s aggregate profits came from one-time gains recorded by First Citizens and Flagstar, which bought the remnants of Silicon Valley Bank (SVB) and Signature Bank, respectively, after they were seized by regulators and sold off at a discount in March.

Even so, the jump in profits also showed US banks in general benefited from rising interest rates, low loan defaults and an expanding job market despite nervousness among depositors and investors.

One way to put this is that most of the other banks did a better job managing interest-rate risk than SVB and First Republic did: Those banks had huge losses on their long-term fixed-rate assets, which swamped any improvement in net interest margins and left them insolvent, while most other banks were a bit more balanced.

But another way to put it is that rising rates are very very bad for a bank if they blow it up , but otherwise they are good: Rising rates reduce the market value of a bank\xe2\x80\x99s fixed-rate assets, which can spook depositors and cause a run and a bank failure, but if depositors ignore the lower market value of the assets then there\xe2\x80\x99s no run and the bank just has higher interest income. These outcomes are discontinuous: Rising rates are either good for a bank, or fatal.

This is not quite right, and a number of banks are facing higher deposit costs and thus lower net interest income , but it is broadly right.\xc2\xa0We talked yesterday about a paper by\xc2\xa0Itamar Drechsler, Alexi Savov, Philipp Schnabl and Olivier Wang, sort of on this dynamic:

Banks hedge the interest rate risk of their assets with their deposit franchise: when interest rates rise, the value of the assets falls but the value of the deposit franchise rises. Yet the deposit franchise is only valuable if depositors remain in the bank. \xe2\x80\xa6

Runs are \xe2\x80\xa6 due to the nature of the deposit franchise. When a deposit is withdrawn, the bank loses the stream of deposit\xc2\xa0spreads net of operating costs it would have earned on that deposit. In effect, the deposit franchise is subject to an extreme form of \xe2\x80\x9cfire sale\xe2\x80\x9d: its value is fully destroyed in a run. Moreover, since its value is increasing in interest rates, a run is more destructive \xe2\x80\x93 and hence more likely \xe2\x80\x93 at high interest rates.

The deposit franchise is both more valuable and more fragile as rates go up. For banks overall, rising rates have been profitable, but for some banks they have been fatal.

Capital call\xc2\xa0financing

One important issue in modern financial markets is settlement time. It used to be that, if you bought a stock from me on a stock exchange, we would meet at the exchange and agree on the trade, and then I would go back to my vault and get my stock certificates, while you would go to your bank and stand in line and fill out a form and get a sack of gold coins to pay me, and then we\xe2\x80\x99d meet back up to hand over the certificates and coins, and we traveled between the exchange and the bank and the vault and the meeting place on foot, and we took long lunches, etc., and so all of this took a week: We\xe2\x80\x99d agree a trade on Monday, and hand over shares and coins the following Monday. Only in the 1990s did US stock markets move to three-business-day (\xe2\x80\x9cT+3\xe2\x80\x9d) settlement . Only this year did the Securities and Exchange Commission\xc2\xa0finalize rules to move to one-business-day (\xe2\x80\x9cT+1\xe2\x80\x9d) settlement; they won\xe2\x80\x99t fully take effect until\xc2\xa0May 2024. The US stock market \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0one of the fastest-moving financial markets \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0still takes two days between agreeing on a trade and actually handing over the money.

People get mad about this delay, but for the most part it is not about, like, archaic computer systems. For the most part it is about financing . If trades settled instantly \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0if you agreed to buy a stock from me on the stock exchange at 12:02 on a Monday, and then we exchanged cash for shares at 12:02:00.001 \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0then everyone would need to pre-fund their accounts. You would need to keep cash somewhere accessible to the exchange at all times, so that as soon as your buy order got filled the money would be there. With T+2 or T+1 settlement, you can agree to do a trade and then go find the money. Maybe you have it invested in Treasury bills to get some yield, and the two-day delay lets you sell the bills to get the cash to buy the stock. Or maybe you need to borrow the money, from a bank or prime broker, to fund the trade. Big market makers and hedge funds are often levered, and someone else advances them the money to pay for their trades. It is helpful to have a day or two to line up that borrowing.

Meanwhile in, like, large-capitalization public-company mergers and acquisitions, settlement delays can be measured in months or years.\xc2\xa0A private equity firm signs a merger agreement to buy a company, and then it has a few months to go out to the leveraged-loan and junk-bond markets and raise the money it needs to pay for the company. [11] Sometimes things go wrong in the interim, and then there are many lawsuits. It would be better, for the seller, to just get the money instantly, but that is not the norm. One reason it is not the norm is that private equity firms do not, like, borrow $20 billion in advance just to have it in their bank account in case they do a deal. It does not make a lot of sense to pre-fund huge investments. First you agree to do a $20 billion leveraged buyout, then you go out and get the $20 billion.

The traditional way that venture capital works is that a VC fund raises commitments from its limited partners (the institutions and people who invest in the fund): When it\xc2\xa0raises a $1 billion fund, investors do not wire it $1 billion immediately to plop into a bank account. Instead each investor says \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99m good for $20 million\xe2\x80\x9d or whatever, and they sign an agreement committing them to put in that amount, and then the investors keep the money and the VC fund goes out looking for investments. And when it finds one, it issues a capital call, and its LPs send in some money, and it uses the money to make the investment. And all of this takes a little time: The LPs have to move the money out of wherever\xc2\xa0they\xe2\x80\x99re\xc2\xa0keeping it\xc2\xa0and wire it to the VC firm, and the VC firm has to put it all together and send another wire to the portfolio company in order to pay for the investment.

And in the recent VC boom I guess people got impatient and it became a competitive advantage for VC firms to say to portfolio companies, like, \xe2\x80\x9cif you sign this termsheet we will wire you the money tomorrow.\xe2\x80\x9d The market demanded shorter settlement times. One way to provide shorter settlement times would be that VC firms could get all the cash for their funds upfront and hold onto it until they are ready to invest: The limited partners could put cash into the fund at the beginning,. This seems inefficient, and limited partners wouldn\xe2\x80\x99t like it.

The more standard and appealing way to do it is with borrowing: VC firms can set up lines of credit with a bank, and when they sign a deal they can call the bank and say \xe2\x80\x9cwe need $100 million to do this deal,\xe2\x80\x9d and the bank\xc2\xa0sends the money that day, and then a week or two later when all the LP money comes in, the VC firm pays back the bank.

This sort of thing is called \xe2\x80\x9ccapital call financing\xe2\x80\x9d or sometimes \xe2\x80\x9csubscription line financing.\xe2\x80\x9d At leveraged buyout firms, this sort of product\xc2\xa0is famously an opportunity for financial gamesmanship . (Basically if the buyout\xc2\xa0firm waits longer to call capital, using the subscription line financing for months or years rather than days or weeks, it can report a higher rate of return on its LPs\xe2\x80\x99 money, because it is using that money for less time.) But even without the gamesmanship this product can be desirable in venture capital, because it satisfies (1) startups\xe2\x80\x99 desire to get cash fast and (2) venture capital limited partners\xe2\x80\x99 desire not to lock up their money before it is needed. Banks, who have lots of money at all times, grease the wheels of VC investing.

But then some VC-friendly banks ran into trouble, which means that this VC-friendly banking product is in trouble. At the Information, Michael Roddan and Kate Clark report :

In the past, these VC firms relied on a specialized credit line offered by SVB and a handful of other banks, including First Republic Bank, that plugged the gap between when the investors, or limited partners, commit capital to VC funds and when that cash actually arrives. But the collapse of both SVB and First Republic is expected to make these loans harder to get. Big banks\xe2\x80\x94such as JPMorgan Chase, which took over First Republic\xe2\x80\x99s business\xe2\x80\x94are wary of extending these loans, known as capital call credit lines, to the small VC firms that need them most but are seen as riskier bets. ...

Unless some banks step in to offer the credit lines, small VC firms may have less ability to move quickly on startup investments, making it harder for them to compete with large firms.

\xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s going to become more and more difficult for smaller VC funds,\xe2\x80\x9d said Dolph Hellman, a partner in San Francisco with Orrick Herrington & Sutcliffe who advises startups and private investors on capital call credit lines. \xe2\x80\xa6

The risk for the banks is that the investor will renege on their commitment, which means the VC firm won\xe2\x80\x99t be able to repay the credit line. As a result, before a bank lends money to a small VC fund, it may want to know who its LPs are, the fund\xe2\x80\x99s track record and how likely it is to be able to convince investors to make good on the capital call. All that work increases the time and cost of dealing with small VC funds, which don\xe2\x80\x99t provide big lenders with a lot of bang for their buck.

\xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s tougher for the banks to get their arms around who the investors in VC funds are. You\xe2\x80\x99re relying on the fact that the LPs are going to honor capital calls. It\xe2\x80\x99s difficult for the banks to determine the creditworthiness of individual investors or family offices,\xe2\x80\x9d Hellman said.

One possibility is that, in a weaker environment for startup investing and venture capital generally, startups will be willing to wait a few weeks to get their VC money?

Things happen

US Default Insurance Cost Eclipses Brazil, Mexico as X-Day Nears. Wall Street Leaders Warn of \xe2\x80\x98Unthinkable\xe2\x80\x99 Fallout If US Defaults. Is picking stocks by throwing darts better than indexing?\xc2\xa0US Antitrust Enforcers Are Chilling Big Mergers. Bonds Backed by Apartments Are Under Stress as Housing Market Cools. The new \xe2\x80\x98blood diamonds\xe2\x80\x99: the elaborate plan to halt Russia\xe2\x80\x99s trade . Saudi Aramco\xe2\x80\x99s Profit Slides as Oil Boom Cools .\xc2\xa0Startups Are Scooping Up Big Tech\xe2\x80\x99s Cast-Off Workers . Jane Street, Jump Pull Back Crypto Trading Over US Regulatory Uncertainty.\xc2\xa0Miami\xe2\x80\x99s Love Affair With Crypto Is Souring as Bitcoin Faithful Flock to the City. Binance slams US crypto crackdown and makes bid for UK oversight. Ex-Coinbase Worker Gets Two-Year Prison Term in First Cryptocurrency Insider-Trading Case. Tucker Carlson Says He\xe2\x80\x99s Launching a New Show on Twitter . Steve Schwarzman Holds Off Giving Money to DeSantis After Meeting Him . [1] I don\xe2\x80\x99t mean to emphasize shareholder value too much here; any form of \xe2\x80\x9cis this best for the company in the long term\xe2\x80\x9d is fine. Some CEOs self-consciously think about customers, not shareholders, etc., but in ways that seem to be good for shareholders in the long run.

[2] If the CEO owns enough super-voting stock, the board is only a temporary constraint: The CEO can fire the board if they try to fire him. (We talk about this dynamic from time to time because it is fun.) Still the board has fiduciary duties to do the right thing for minority shareholders, even if it gets them fired.

[3] I am ignoring some legal technicalities here because even I \xe2\x80\x94 a guy who is pretty okay with legal technicalities \xe2\x80\x94 find them boring. Basically, as far as I dimly remember from law school (don\xe2\x80\x99t quote me on this), the way it works is that directors have fiduciary duties to their company, but only the company can sue them for breaching those duties. So if some director is taking bribes or whatever, the other directors can vote to have the company sue her. But if *all* of the directors are breaching their fiduciary duties, then obviously they won\xe2\x80\x99t vote to sue themselves. And so the law allows shareholders to bring \xe2\x80\x9cderivative suits\xe2\x80\x9d on behalf of the company, but only if they can show that the company wouldn\xe2\x80\x99t bring the lawsuit itself because the directors are too conflicted. This requirement is called, for somehow even more boring reasons, \xe2\x80\x9c demand futility ,\xe2\x80\x9d and it \xe2\x80\x9chinges on whether the plaintiff adequately alleges that the [board] members would face a substantial likelihood of liability for approving the transaction.\xe2\x80\x9d (Another technicality has to do with the exculpatory provisions in the company\xe2\x80\x99s charter: \xe2\x80\x9cWhere, as here, the corporation\xe2\x80\x99s certificate of incorporation exculpates its directors from liability to the fullest extent permitted by law, the substantial-likelihood standard requires that a plaintiff \xe2\x80\x98plead particularized facts providing a reason to believe that the individual director was self-interested, beholden to an interested party, or acted in bad faith.\xe2\x80\x99\xe2\x80\x9d)

[4] These quotes are me quoting Chancellor McCormick quoting a 2006 Chancery Court decision, In re The Walt Disney Co. Derivative Litigation.

[5] Incidentally, I write a lot around here about how \xe2\x80\x9ceverything is securities fraud\xe2\x80\x9d: Every bad thing that happens to a public company can be characterized as securities fraud, because shareholders bought the stock thinking that the bad thing wouldn\xe2\x80\x99t happen, so they can claim they were deceived by the company\xe2\x80\x99s disclosures. If the Delaware rule is, kind of, \xe2\x80\x9cnothing is a breach of fiduciary duties,\xe2\x80\x9d then surely the right move for shareholder lawsuits is to sue for securities fraud instead? \xe2\x80\x9cYou didn\xe2\x80\x99t disclose in your risk factors that Jack Dorsey was hanging out with Jay-Z, so your stock was overvalued,\xe2\x80\x9d etc.

[6] To be clear the *reason* to be conservative is that you are going to have absurdly good earnings this quarter, so they sort of don\xe2\x80\x99t matter: First Citizens reported net income of $271 million in the first quarter of 2022, $257 million in the fourth quarter of 2022, and $9.52 billion in the first quarter of 2023, almost all of which was gain on acquisition. (\xe2\x80\x9cAdjusted net income available to common shareholders,\xe2\x80\x9d ignoring the gain on acquisition, was a more sensible $292 million.) Nobody is updating their financial model to assume that First Citizens will have $9.52 billion of quarterly income from now on; that number is sort of meaningless. But if you mark the assets very conservatively now, and they end up being worth more than your marks, your meaningless one-time income is lower today, but you have positive income in *future* quarters, when you want it. If you mark the assets pretty high now, and they end up being worth less later, then your meaningless one-time income is higher today but your future earnings are lower. Earnings from SVB\xe2\x80\x99s assets in future quarters are worth way, way, way more to First Citizens than earnings from those assets today. Counterbalancing this incentive is that, like, your accounting has to be correct, you have third-party experts reviewing it, etc. But the incentives are to mark stuff down.

[7] Slide 19 of the earnings presentation walks through the math: A $16.45 billion discount on the assets, minus about $2.8 billion of adjustments to the asset values, minus a $500 million cash payment to the FDIC, gets you to $13.1 billion of actual gains. Subtract $3.3 billion of deferred tax liability and you get $9.8 billion.

[8] The lower number here comes largely from loan loss provisions on the SVB assets that do not get counted in the gain-on-acquisition math.

[9] Again, see slide 19 of the presentation : $110.1 billion of assets minus $2.8 billion of adjustments gets you about $107.3 billion of assets at fair values. And the price was $93.6 billion of assumed deposits, plus $500 million of top-up payment to the FDIC, or $94.1 billion total. This is a little casual, and in fact some of the purchase price adjustments are not strictly about the fair value of SVB\xe2\x80\x99s *assets*. For instance there\xe2\x80\x99s a $230 million benefit for the \xe2\x80\x9ccore deposit intangible,\xe2\x80\x9d that is, sort of, the deposit franchise. And First Citizens takes a $220 million benefit from \xe2\x80\x9cdiscount on purchase money note\xe2\x80\x9d: Part of the assumed liabilities was a $35 billion loan from the FDIC to First Citizens to provide long-term funding for some of the SVB loans, and that loan was at below-market rates, creating an additional benefit for First Citizens.

[10] See page 6 of the earnings release .

[11] I\xe2\x80\x99m being a bit cute; most of the delay here is caused by the need to get regulatory approvals and a shareholder vote. If those were instantaneous, you\xe2\x80\x99d probably see the financing markets adapt by getting quicker.
